title: Hyperfine-resolved laser excitation and detection of nuclear isomer in trapped $^{229}$Th$^{3+}$ ions
authors: Wu Wang, Ke Zhang, Ke-Mi Xu, Shan-Gui Zhou
abstract: We present a comprehensive theoretical investigation of hyperfine-resolved excitation and detection of the low-energy isomeric state of $^{229}$Th in trapped $^{229}\mathrm{Th}^{3+}$ ions. Using a quantum master equation approach, we analyze the dependence of the isomeric population on laser linewidth, detuning, and irradiation time, showing that their proper matching is essential for efficient excitation. We further propose two nuclear-state detection schemes based on three hyperfine-resolved electronic fluorescence channels at 690, 984, and 1088 nm. Our analysis shows that the 690-nm and 984-nm scheme yields detectable photon rates on the order of $10^4~\mathrm{s}^{-1}$ per ion for each wavelength, whereas the 1088-nm scheme achieves a higher rate on the order of $10^5~\mathrm{s}^{-1}$ per ion. By quantifying the trade-off between irradiation time and scan-step size, we show that the nuclear transition can be located within one month for a 100-MHz uncertainty using currently available vacuum-ultraviolet laser technology. These results provide practical guidance for trapped-ion $^{229}\mathrm{Th}$ spectroscopy and the development of nuclear clocks.
summary: This paper presents a theoretical framework for the laser excitation and detection of the 229Th nuclear isomer in trapped ions. It proposes specific electronic fluorescence channels to improve detection efficiency and demonstrates that the nuclear transition can be found relatively quickly using current VUV technology. This work is a significant step toward the realization of practical nuclear clocks.
main_problem: Achieving efficient laser excitation and detection of the low-energy nuclear isomeric state of 229Th in trapped-ion systems, specifically overcoming low fluorescence signals and large frequency uncertainties.
main_result: The study identifies that matching laser linewidth, detuning, and irradiation time is essential for excitation and proposes that a 1088-nm detection scheme provides the highest photon rate, enabling the nuclear transition to be located within one month.
method: A quantum master equation approach in the Lindblad form is used to analyze hyperfine-resolved excitation dynamics and population transfer efficiency.
model_or_system: The system consists of 229Th3+ ions in a trap, modeling the nuclear transition between ground and isomeric states via an M1 transition and electronic transitions via E1 transitions using a hyperfine-coupled basis.
key_observables: Isomeric population, electronic fluorescence photon rates (690, 984, and 1088 nm), and total search time for the nuclear transition frequency.
important_parameters: Nuclear Rabi frequency (~3.4 Hz), laser linewidths (100 Hz to 10 MHz), nuclear isomeric lifetime (~2500 s), and transition energy (~8.4 eV).
main_assumptions: The nuclear isomer decay is neglected for short time scales (up to 500 s) to treat the system as a two-level model, and the rotating-wave approximation is applied.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows nuclear and electronic energy levels and proposed fluorescence wavelengths; Figure 2 displays hyperfine structures; Figure 10 shows electronic population as a function of laser detuning.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the scientific problem, describes the physical system and hyperfine model, details the quantum master equation methodology, presents results for excitation and detection schemes, and concludes with an optimization of the nuclear transition search time.
why_interesting: It provides critical theoretical guidance for the development of nuclear clocks and explores the control of hyperfine-resolved dynamics in a system coupling nuclear and electronic degrees of freedom.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: (a) Nuclear ground and isomeric levels of 229Th, connected by an M1 transition with energy hν ≈8.4 eV. The vacuum radiative lifetime of the isomer is about 2500 s. (b) Partial low-lying electronic levels of 229Th3+, with 5f5/2 as the electronic ground level. The 690-nm, 984-nm, and 1088-nm transitions are used in the proposed electronic-ﬂuorescence detection schemes.

FIG. 2: Hyperﬁne structures of the selected electronic 5f5/2, 5f7/2, 6d3/2, and 6d5/2 levels in 229Th3+ and 229mTh3+. The hyperﬁne splittings are given in units of MHz. Hyperﬁne-resolved laser excitation is essential for improving the precision of the isomeric energy, while the distinct hyperﬁne structures of the nuclear ground and isomeric states enable nuclear-state detection via hyperﬁne-resolved electronic ﬂuorescence.

FIG. 4: Three-level detection scheme based on the 690-nm and 984-nm electronic transitions in the nuclear-ground-state conﬁguration. (a) With only the 690-nm laser applied, the electronic population is driven from states |a⟩to |c⟩via the intermediate state |b⟩, accompanied by the emission of 984-nm ﬂuorescence. The states |a⟩, |b⟩, and |c⟩belong to the hyperﬁne levels (Ig, 5f5/2, F = 1), (Ig, 6d5/2, F = 0), and (Ig, 5f7/2, F = 1), respectively. (b) With only the 984-nm laser applied, conversely, the electronic population is driven from |c⟩back to |a⟩via |b⟩, resulting in the emission of 690-nm ﬂuorescence. The 690-nm or 984-nm ﬂuorescence is suppressed when the nuclear isomer is excited.

FIG. 6: Isomeric population of the state |2⟩as a function of irradiation time (a) at zero detuning ∆n,l/2π = 0 kHz for diﬀerent laser linewidths γn,l, and (b) for diﬀerent detunings ∆n,l with the laser linewidth ﬁxed at γn,l/2π = 10 kHz. In panel (b), the circled curves represent the analytic solutions in Eq. (13), while the uncircled curves correspond to the nu- merical solutions of Eq. (4). The intensity of the 148-nm laser is ﬁxed at 0.5 W/cm2 and the system is initially prepared in the state |1⟩for all cases.

FIG. 5: Two-level detection scheme based on the 1088-nm electronic transition in the nuclear-isomeric-state conﬁgura- tion. The laser couples the states |a′⟩and |b′⟩, which belong to the hyperﬁne levels (Ie, 5f5/2, F = 1) and (Ie, 6d3/2, F = 0), respectively. The 1088-nm ﬂuorescence is enhanced when the nuclear isomer is excited.

FIG. 7: Quasi-steady-state population of the isomeric state |2⟩as a function of the laser detuning ∆n,l (a) for diﬀerent laser linewidths, and (b) for diﬀerent irradiation times, as labeled. In panel (a), the irradiation time is set to 20 and 200 s for γn,l/2π = 1 and 10 kHz, respectively. In panel (b), the laser linewidth is ﬁxed at γn,l/2π = 100 Hz. The intensity of the 148-nm laser is set to 0.5 W/cm2 and the system is initially prepared in the state |1⟩for all cases.

FIG. 8: Electronic population of the state |c⟩as a function of irradiation time (a) at a ﬁxed detuning ∆e,l/2π = 100 MHz for diﬀerent laser linewidths γe,l, and (b) for diﬀerent detun- ings ∆e,l with the laser linewidth ﬁxed at γe,l/2π = 0.1 MHz. In all cases, the electronic population transfer is driven by a 690-nm laser with an intensity of 1 W/cm2. The system is initially prepared in the state |a⟩.

FIG. 9: Electronic population of the state |a⟩as a function of irradiation time (a) at a ﬁxed detuning ∆e,l/2π = 100 MHz for diﬀerent laser linewidths γe,l, and (b) for diﬀerent detun- ings ∆e,l with the laser linewidth ﬁxed at γe,l/2π = 0.1 MHz. In all cases, the electronic population transfer is driven by a 984-nm laser with an intensity of 0.1 W/cm2. The system is initially prepared in the state |c⟩.

FIG. 10: Electronic population of the state |b′⟩as a function of the 1088-nm laser detuning ∆′ e,l for diﬀerent laser intensi- ties, as labeled. The irradiation time is set to 10, 5.9, and 4.6 ns for laser intensities of 10, 30, and 50 W/cm2, respectively. The system is initially prepared in the state |a′⟩and the laser linewidth is ﬁxed at γ′ e,l/2π = 1 MHz for all cases.
